head.hi: The Ambassador in Japan (Forbes) to the Secretary of State

div.p: 74. The British Military Attaché has just arrived from Shanghai with very interesting details, indicating that the Japanese advanced about 5 kilometers in the open region north of Shanghai but that very little advance was made in the thickly settled part of the city and it is anticipated they will continue their movements more in the open region outside. It is reported that the Chinese are offering stiff opposition in the city.
A report is just in that after a meeting of the Cabinet it was decided to send the 11th Division to Shanghai. This was requested by their Minister and Consul General and not by General Uyeda.

I saw the French, German, British and Italian Ambassadors yesterday. From all sources it is evident that the Japanese are very anxious to terminate the Shanghai situation at the earliest possible moment. It seems that if the fighting could be stopped the Japanese would not insist on a very extensive withdrawal on the part of the Chinese. Discussing the Manchurian situation, it was the opinion of my colleagues that independent Manchuria state was inadmissible and could not be recognized by the powers; that there might be an autonomous local government set up in Manchuria but the customs schedules and general laws of China would be in force there and the territories of course recognized as Chinese.
Nagai tells Neville that in his opinion an independent Manchuria is impracticable.
